title: IndiGo, Air India Slash Domestic Seats as Fares Climb
excerpt: IndiGo, Air India and Air India Express are cutting domestic capacity amid the West Asia fuel crisis, pushing fares higher. Here's how to book smart.
content:
If your domestic fares have looked unusually steep this summer, it isn't your imagination. India's three biggest full-service and low-cost carriers are quietly flying fewer seats than they were a year ago, and the gap is showing up directly in ticket prices on some of the country's busiest corridors.

According to aviation data firm OAG, IndiGo carried 11.6 million domestic seats in July 2026, still more than half of India's total capacity but down slightly from a year earlier. Air India's cuts are far steeper: its domestic capacity is down 19.2 percent year-on-year, roughly 659,000 fewer seats, and Air India Express has trimmed capacity by 17 percent, about 445,000 seats. Route-level data from AeroRoutes shows Air India has suspended flights on Delhi-Goa (Manohar International) and Mumbai-Nagpur, while Air India Express has pulled out of Hyderabad-Pune beyond July 31 and cut services to Ayodhya and Mangalore. IndiGo's reductions are more modest at around 6 percent of capacity, with Navi Mumbai-Durgapur and Navi Mumbai-Jamnagar suspended. SpiceJet has been cutting capacity even longer, down roughly 17.5 percent with about 20 routes suspended earlier this year, including services out of Mumbai and Chennai.

The common thread behind these cuts is the same West Asia crisis that has been disrupting international Gulf routes for weeks: elevated jet fuel costs, airspace workarounds, and weaker post-peak-summer demand are all squeezing airline economics at once. There was a small bright spot on July 1, when Air India cut fuel surcharges on long-haul international routes to North America and Europe and the price of aviation turbine fuel dropped by about Rs 5 per litre to roughly Rs 110. But domestic flying hasn't seen the same relief. Air India still applies a distance-based domestic fuel surcharge of Rs 299 to Rs 899 per ticket depending on how far you're flying, and industry trackers report fares on some routes running 20 to 40 percent above off-season levels, with spikes of up to 30 percent on the most affected corridors. Delhi-Mumbai, Bangalore-Delhi and Mumbai-Bangalore, the three heaviest trunk routes in the country, are seeing the tightest capacity and the biggest price pressure.

One carrier is moving in the opposite direction. Akasa Air is the only major Indian airline adding capacity this summer, up 7.4 percent in July to 881,000 seats, after adding roughly 157,000 seats across June and July combined. The airline is raising about Rs 1,050 crore in fresh capital and has said it plans a 30 percent capacity increase for the financial year ending March 2027, backed by new aircraft deliveries and improved pilot availability. Where its routes overlap with IndiGo's, Akasa has generally been pricing 5 to 15 percent below it. There's also some route churn worth knowing about if you fly through Noida International Airport: Akasa is set to resume its Noida-Navi Mumbai service from October 1, while IndiGo has suspended its Noida-Chandigarh flight.

What should you actually do? First, if you're booking Delhi-Mumbai, Bangalore-Delhi, Mumbai-Bangalore or any route currently served by Air India, Air India Express or SpiceJet, book sooner rather than later — capacity on these routes is measurably tighter than it was a year ago, and there's no sign of an imminent reversal since the underlying driver is the broader Gulf fuel and airspace situation. Second, if your itinerary allows flexibility on carrier, check whether Akasa Air flies your route; it's the one airline actively adding seats and has generally undercut IndiGo's pricing where the two compete head-to-head. Third, factor Air India's distance-based domestic fuel surcharge into your budget when comparing fares across airlines, since it isn't always broken out clearly at checkout. And if your travel plans route through Noida airport, double-check your specific city pair against the latest airline schedule rather than assuming last month's flight list still holds, since both IndiGo and Akasa have been actively reshuffling that network. None of this points to a full-blown fare crisis, but the direction of travel through the rest of the summer is up, not down, so booking early is the safer bet.
